planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist to consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; pendulous sedge thrives in wet, shaded conditions beside ponds, streams, and in rain gardens where few ornamental grasses perform.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; pendulous sedge (weeping sedge) is a large, bold, evergreen European sedge that forms impressive arching clumps of broad, strap-like, bright-green leaves, with long, pendulous flower spikes that arch gracefully outward and downward in late spring and summer — the elongated, cylindrical brown catkin-like flower spikes dangling from arching stems are one of the most distinctive and structurally elegant features in the shade garden. It thrives in wet, shaded conditions beside ponds and streams where few ornamental grasses can grow. The dense, lush foliage creates dramatic textural contrast with broadleaf shade plants like hostas and astilbes. Can self-sow prolifically in ideal moist conditions; remove seed heads before they mature if unwanted seedlings are a concern. One of the most architecturally impressive sedges for large-scale shade and water garden compositions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in spring; Gray's sedge grows naturally in wet woodland edges and stream-side meadows and requires consistently moist to wet conditions — it will not thrive in dry soil.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Gray's sedge (morningstar sedge, mace sedge) is a native North American woodland sedge grown primarily for its extraordinarily ornamental seed heads — large, spiky, star-shaped, mace-like fruiting heads that look remarkably like medieval weapons, held on upright stems above the bright-green, arching foliage clumps from summer through early fall. The seed head is the plant's primary ornamental feature and is excellent for both fresh and dried flower arrangements. The plant provides attractive foliage mass in moist, shaded corners throughout the growing season. It grows naturally in wet woodland edges and floodplain forests and is most at home beside streams, ponds, and rain garden margins in partial shade. An excellent specimen plant for rain gardens, bioswales, and wet native plant gardens in zones 4–9.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; tufted hair grass is one of the few ornamental grasses that performs well in partial shade and is especially effective in moist, partially shaded garden settings where most grasses fail.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; tufted hair grass is one of the most graceful and versatile of cool-season ornamental grasses, forming dense, dark-green, fine-textured tufts of narrow, arching leaves that produce extraordinary airy clouds of delicate, silvery-tan, open-panicle flower heads on slender, arching stems in early summer — when backlit by morning or evening sun, the massed flower heads create a luminous, misty, gossamer effect that is among the most beautiful of all grass effects in the garden. It is distinctively shade-tolerant compared to most ornamental grasses and thrives in moist, cool, partially shaded sites. The flower heads dry in place and provide multi-season interest through winter. 'Goldtau' (gold-tinted), 'Schottland' (tall, graceful), and 'Bronzeschleier' (bronze veil) are superb cultivars. Excellent with hostas and astilbes in moist shade gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; purple moor grass is a slow-establishing but long-lived grass that improves significantly after the second or third year in the garden — patience is rewarded.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; purple moor grass is a European native of wet heathlands and moors that is grown in the ornamental garden for its exceptional fall color — the foliage and flower stems turn warm golden-orange to amber-yellow in autumn, creating one of the most brilliant grass color displays available. The slender, arching stems bear delicate, purplish flower panicles in summer that arch gracefully and move freely in the breeze. Plants die completely to the ground in winter, making spring clean-up easy (simply pull the old clumps away). Slow to establish but forms handsome, non-invasive clumps that improve over years. 'Variegata' (cream-striped leaves), 'Heidebraut' (upright, tall), and the large cultivar group Molinia caerulea ssp. arundinacea provides taller, more dramatic forms to 5–6 feet. An excellent grass for moist, acidic, partially shaded sites.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; wood melic grass grows naturally in shaded woodland on alkaline or neutral soils and is specifically suited to shade conditions — it does not perform well in full sun.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; wood melic grass is a European woodland grass that is one of the few true ornamental grasses suited to deep shade — it forms loose, delicate tufts of soft, flat, rich-green leaves and produces loose, arching panicles of nodding, dark-purple to bronze, single-spikeleted flowers from late spring through early summer that glisten with a silky, metallic quality when backlit. The overall effect of wood melic in flower is ethereal and refined — far more subtle and naturalistic than most ornamental grasses. Excellent for naturalizing as a groundcover in shaded woodland gardens under deciduous trees and in formal shade borders where a soft, grassy textural element is needed without the full-sun requirements of most grasses. The cultivar 'Variegata' (cream-striped leaves) is slightly more ornamental. Self-sows moderately in suitable shaded, humus-rich conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
